name: Data pipeline / analytics / ETL
summary: A system that ingests, transforms, and delivers data across batch and/or streaming stages into warehouses, lakes, or downstream consumers. Correctness under reprocessing, data quality, schema change, and governance dominate over interactive UX.

concerns: data-quality dimensions with enforced thresholds: accuracy, completeness, consistency, uniqueness, validity, timeliness/freshness
schema evolution and data contracts: backward/forward compatibility, additive-only vs breaking changes, schema registry
validation and quarantine of bad records (dead-letter/reject tables) rather than silent drop or pipeline halt
data lineage and provenance: source-to-target traceability, transformation documentation, impact analysis
partitioning, layout (bronze/silver/gold), and file/format choice (Parquet/Avro/Iceberg/Delta) with compaction
reference-data and slowly-changing-dimension handling; deduplication and merge keys
late-arriving, out-of-order, and corrected/restated data handling
questions: Are data-quality dimensions and their enforced thresholds (freshness, completeness, accuracy) specified?
Is schema evolution governed by data contracts with defined compatibility rules and a handling path for breaking changes?
Are bad records validated and quarantined (not silently dropped), and is lineage/provenance captured?

concerns: idempotent, deterministic transforms so a re-run produces identical output (partition overwrite / MERGE / dedup)
exactly-once vs at-least-once + idempotent-consumer semantics, explicitly chosen per stage
backfill and replay: re-processing historical windows correctly regardless of existing data, without double counting
checkpointing, offset management, and recovery of streaming state after failure
failure isolation: a bad batch/partition fails without corrupting downstream or blocking unrelated data
watermarking and completeness signals so consumers know when a partition is final
questions: Is idempotency and the delivery semantics (exactly-once vs at-least-once) specified per stage so re-runs don't double-count?
Is a backfill/replay strategy defined that reprocesses history correctly without duplication?
Is streaming-state checkpointing/recovery and partition-level failure isolation specified?

concerns: transformation/business-logic correctness and its testability against known input/output fixtures
batch vs streaming (or unified/lambda-kappa) processing semantics per dataset
aggregation, windowing (tumbling/sliding/session), and join correctness across sources
CDC capture semantics (inserts/updates/deletes, tombstones) and merge into targets
handling of corrections/restatements and their propagation downstream
questions: Are transformation rules specified precisely enough to test against known input/output fixtures?
Are windowing, aggregation, and join semantics across sources defined?
Is CDC/merge behaviour for updates and deletes (tombstones) specified?

concerns: orchestration and scheduling (DAGs, dependencies, sensors, triggers) with retry, backoff, and SLA/miss alerting
data-freshness/SLA monitoring, pipeline health, and lag/backpressure alerting
observability of data (volume anomalies, null-rate drift, distribution shift) beyond system metrics
operational runbooks for backfills, replays, and stuck/failed jobs; on-call for data incidents
environment/config management and safe promotion of pipeline/transform code
questions: Is orchestration with dependency management, retry/backoff, and SLA-miss alerting specified?
Is data-freshness/lag monitoring and data-anomaly observability (volume, null-rate, drift) defined?
Are runbooks for backfill/replay and failed-job recovery specified?

concerns: throughput and end-to-end latency targets (batch window completion; streaming p95 event-to-sink lag)
scalability with data volume growth; partitioning/parallelism and skew/hot-key handling
streaming backpressure, buffering, and consumer-lag bounds
resource and compute-cost efficiency (shuffle, small-files, over-partitioning, scan cost) with cost budgets
incremental processing over full recompute to bound cost and time
questions: Are throughput and latency/lag targets quantified (batch window, streaming event-to-sink)?
Is scaling with volume growth and data-skew/hot-key handling specified?
Are compute-cost budgets and incremental-vs-full-recompute strategy defined?

concerns: testability of transforms: unit tests on logic, data tests/assertions (dbt tests, Great Expectations) in-pipeline
modular, reusable, version-controlled transformations and DAGs; environment parity
documentation of datasets, contracts, and semantics (data catalog); discoverability
reproducibility and deterministic rebuilds from source; versioned datasets/transform code
impact analysis for schema/logic changes across downstream consumers
questions: Are automated transform unit tests and in-pipeline data-quality assertions required?
Are datasets, contracts, and semantics documented (catalog) for discoverability?
Is reproducibility (deterministic rebuild from source, versioned code) specified?

concerns: PII governance across the pipeline: classification, masking/tokenization/pseudonymization, and column-level access control
GDPR/CCPA data-subject rights over derived/aggregated data: erasure/right-to-be-forgotten propagation through lakes and backups
purpose limitation and consent-scope enforcement on downstream analytics use
data residency/sovereignty of processing and storage locations
retention and defensible deletion of raw and derived datasets; audit of data access
questions: Is PII classified, masked/tokenized, and access-controlled at column level through the pipeline?
Is erasure/right-to-be-forgotten propagated to derived datasets, lakes, and backups?
Are data-residency and retention/deletion rules specified for raw and derived data?

concerns: least-privilege access to sources, sinks, and intermediate storage; per-dataset/role access control
encryption in transit and at rest across staging, lake, and warehouse; key management
secrets/credential management for source/sink connectors (no plaintext in DAG code)
integrity/authenticity of ingested data and protection against poisoned/malicious input
isolation of tenant/domain data in shared processing infrastructure
questions: Is least-privilege, per-dataset access control across sources/sinks/intermediates specified?
Is encryption at rest and in transit across all stages, with key and connector-secret management, defined?
Is ingested-data integrity and multi-tenant isolation addressed?

concerns: heterogeneous source/sink connectors (DBs, APIs, files, queues, SaaS) and their format/protocol variance
serialization formats and their schema-registry compatibility (Avro/Protobuf/JSON) across producers and consumers
downstream consumer contracts: stable dataset interfaces so consumers don't break on change
interoperability with orchestration, catalog, and BI tools; open table formats
questions: Are the source/sink connectors, formats, and protocols specified with their compatibility rules?
Are stable consumer-facing dataset contracts defined so downstream consumers don't break on change?

concerns: compute/storage cost ceilings and budget as a first-class constraint (warehouse credits, egress, cluster hours)
mandated platform/stack (e.g. specific warehouse, orchestrator, cloud) and its limits
processing-window / SLA deadlines constraining architecture (batch cutoffs)
open-source licensing of pipeline components and data-usage/license constraints on source data
questions: Are compute/storage cost ceilings captured as explicit constraints?
Are mandated platforms/tools and processing-window/SLA deadlines specified?
Are licensing constraints on components and source data addressed?
